1.1.1.fail: A screen reader reaches the image and announces only the file name — "newsletter dot gif, image" — so the user has no idea what it shows.
1.1.1.pass: The screen reader reads the alt text aloud — "Free newsletter. Get free recipes and more." — giving the user the same information a sighted user sees.

1.2.1.fail: A Deaf user opens the podcast and finds only an audio player — no words to read — so the content is simply unavailable to them.
1.2.1.pass: A transcript link sits beside the player, so a Deaf user reads the full content and a blind user gets a silent video described in text.

1.2.2.fail: A hard-of-hearing viewer presses play and sees lips moving with no captions — the dialogue and a doorbell sound pass by unheard.
1.2.2.pass: Synchronized captions appear in time with the speech, so the viewer follows every line of dialogue and meaningful sound.

1.2.3.fail: A blind user hears the soundtrack but misses an on-screen chart the narrator never mentions, losing key information.
1.2.3.pass: An audio description narrates the visuals during pauses (or a full text alternative is provided), so the blind user gets the on-screen information too.

1.2.4.fail: A Deaf attendee joins the live webinar and the speech goes by with no real-time text — they can't follow along as it happens.
1.2.4.pass: Live captions stream in near-real-time, so the Deaf attendee follows the webinar as it unfolds.

1.2.5.fail: A blind viewer follows the dialogue, but a silent on-screen demonstration leaves a gap they can't fill.
1.2.5.pass: An audio-description track narrates the key visuals, so the blind viewer experiences the full video.

1.2.6.fail: A Deaf user whose first language is a sign language must rely on written captions, reading slower and missing nuance.
1.2.6.pass: A picture-in-picture interpreter signs the audio, giving the user content in their native language.

1.2.7.fail: The video's pauses are too short, so the audio description is cut off and the blind viewer misses fast-moving visual detail.
1.2.7.pass: The video pauses to let extended descriptions finish, so the blind viewer captures every visual detail.

1.2.8.fail: A Deafblind user on a braille display reaches the video and finds nothing their device can render.
1.2.8.pass: A full text alternative — dialogue plus visual description — feeds the braille display, making the video fully accessible.

1.2.9.fail: A Deaf listener tunes into the live audio stream and gets silence with no accompanying text.
1.2.9.pass: A live text feed runs alongside the audio, so the Deaf listener follows the broadcast in real time.

1.3.1.fail: A screen reader announces a styled <div> as plain text, so the user never learns it was a heading, list, or table — the structure is invisible to them.
1.3.1.pass: Semantic markup lets the screen reader announce "heading level 2", "list, 5 items", or a labeled field, so the user perceives the same structure sighted users see.

1.3.2.fail: A screen reader reads columns in DOM order that CSS has visually reordered, so the steps come out scrambled.
1.3.2.pass: The reading order matches the visual order, so the screen reader narrates content in a sensible sequence.

1.3.3.fail: The instruction says "press the round green button on the right" — a blind user has no way to identify which control that is.
1.3.3.pass: The instruction names the control — "press the Submit button" — so it's identifiable without seeing shape, color, or position.

1.3.4.fail: A user with a wheelchair-mounted tablet can't rotate it, and the app shows a blank "please rotate your device" wall.
1.3.4.pass: The layout works in both portrait and landscape, so a user with a fixed-mount device can use it either way.

1.3.5.fail: A user with a cognitive disability faces a bare phone field that won't autofill and shows no familiar icon — every field is manual effort.
1.3.5.pass: autocomplete="tel" lets the browser autofill and assistive tools add a familiar icon, cutting the effort.

1.3.6.fail: A personalization tool can't tell which region is navigation or which icon means "home", so it can't simplify the interface.
1.3.6.pass: Landmarks and ARIA expose each region and control's purpose, so personalization tools can adapt the UI.

1.4.1.fail: A colorblind user sees required fields marked only in red and can't tell them apart from the rest.
1.4.1.pass: Required fields also carry an icon or text label, so the cue survives colorblindness and grayscale.

1.4.2.fail: Autoplaying audio blares over a blind user's screen reader, drowning out its speech until they can find a stop control.
1.4.2.pass: A visible pause/stop control (or no autoplay) lets the user silence the audio so their screen reader can be heard.

1.4.3.fail: A user with low vision squints at light-gray text on white and gives up — the words don't separate from the background.
1.4.3.pass: Text meets a 4.5:1 contrast ratio, so a low-vision user reads it without strain.

1.4.4.fail: A low-vision user zooms to 200% and the text overlaps and clips, breaking the layout.
1.4.4.pass: At 200% zoom the text reflows cleanly with no loss of content or function.

1.4.5.fail: A low-vision user enlarges a heading that's actually a PNG and it turns into a blurry, pixelated smear.
1.4.5.pass: Real text stays crisp at any zoom and can be recolored or read aloud.

1.4.6.fail: A user with moderately low vision can make out AA-level text only with effort and can't read it comfortably.
1.4.6.pass: Text meets the stronger 7:1 ratio, so a moderately low-vision user reads it comfortably without assistive tech.

1.4.7.fail: A hard-of-hearing listener can't separate the speaker's voice from loud background music.
1.4.7.pass: Background sound stays well below the speech (or can be turned off), so the voice stays clear.

1.4.8.fail: A user with dyslexia loses their place in justified, full-width, tightly-spaced text.
1.4.8.pass: Narrow, left-aligned, generously-spaced text lets the user track each line.

1.4.9.fail: A screen reader skips words baked into an image, and a low-vision user can't resize or recolor them.
1.4.9.pass: All meaningful text is real text — readable, resizable, and recolorable.

1.4.10.fail: A low-vision user at 400% zoom must scroll left and right on every single line to read.
1.4.10.pass: Content reflows into one column, so the user scrolls in only one direction.

1.4.11.fail: A low-vision user can't find the input field — its faint border blends into the page background.
1.4.11.pass: Borders, icons, and focus rings meet 3:1 contrast, so the user can locate controls and their states.

1.4.12.fail: A user bumps up line and letter spacing to read more easily, and the text overflows its button and gets clipped.
1.4.12.pass: Increased text spacing is absorbed gracefully, with no clipping or overlap.

1.4.13.fail: A zoomed-in user moves toward a tooltip to read it and it vanishes the moment the pointer leaves the trigger.
1.4.13.pass: The tooltip stays put, can be hovered, and dismisses with Escape — so the user can actually read it.

2.1.1.fail: A keyboard-only user tabs through the page but can't reach or activate a div-based "button" — the action is unavailable.
2.1.1.pass: Every control is reachable with Tab and operable with Enter or Space, so the keyboard user can do everything.

2.1.2.fail: A keyboard user tabs into a widget and can't tab back out — focus is stuck and the rest of the page is unreachable.
2.1.2.pass: Focus moves in and back out with standard keys, so the user is never trapped.

2.1.3.fail: A keyboard-only user hits a drawing or drag feature with no key-operable alternative and is blocked.
2.1.3.pass: Every feature, including drag and draw, has a keyboard path.

2.1.4.fail: A speech-input user dictates a word, a stray single-key shortcut fires, and it deletes their work.
2.1.4.pass: Single-key shortcuts can be turned off, remapped, or only fire when focused — no accidental triggers.

2.2.1.fail: A user who reads slowly is logged out mid-form with no warning and loses everything they typed.
2.2.1.pass: The user is warned before the timeout and can extend it, so they finish at their own pace.

2.2.2.fail: A user with ADHD can't focus on the article because a carousel keeps auto-rotating beside it with no off switch.
2.2.2.pass: A pause control lets the user stop the motion and concentrate.

2.2.3.fail: A user who needs extra time is rushed by a countdown that auto-submits before they finish.
2.2.3.pass: There's no time limit, so the user takes as long as they need.

2.2.4.fail: A screen reader user loses their place as a notification keeps interrupting and shifting focus.
2.2.4.pass: The user can mute or defer notifications and stay focused.

2.2.5.fail: A user who stepped away is logged out and returns to find the long form they'd filled in wiped clean.
2.2.5.pass: After re-authenticating, their data is preserved and they continue where they left off.

2.2.6.fail: A user takes a break unaware that inactivity will silently discard their work.
2.2.6.pass: The user is told up front how long they can be idle before any data is lost.

2.3.1.fail: A rapidly strobing banner risks triggering a seizure in a user with photosensitive epilepsy.
2.3.1.pass: Nothing flashes more than three times a second, so the content is seizure-safe.

2.3.2.fail: Even a small but fast-flashing indicator could trigger a seizure in a highly photosensitive user.
2.3.2.pass: No content flashes more than three times a second, regardless of size.

2.3.3.fail: A user with a vestibular disorder feels dizzy as large parallax motion fires on scroll with no way to turn it off.
2.3.3.pass: Honoring the reduce-motion setting, the motion is removed so the user isn't made ill.

2.4.1.fail: A screen reader user must tab through the same 30 navigation links on every page before reaching the content.
2.4.1.pass: A skip link (or landmarks) jumps the user straight to the main content.

2.4.2.fail: A screen reader user with many tabs open hears the same generic title on each and can't tell pages apart.
2.4.2.pass: Each page announces a unique, descriptive title on load, so the user knows where they are.

2.4.3.fail: Tabbing jumps erratically — last name, then first name, then submit — confusing the keyboard user.
2.4.3.pass: Focus moves in a logical order matching the visual layout.

2.4.4.fail: A screen reader user pulls up the links list and hears "click here, click here, read more" with no idea where any goes.
2.4.4.pass: Each link's text describes its destination, so the links list is meaningful.

2.4.5.fail: A user who can't navigate the menu has no search or sitemap and can't find the page they need.
2.4.5.pass: Search plus navigation gives the user more than one way to reach any page.

2.4.6.fail: A screen reader user jumps by heading and hears only "Section 2" — no clue what's in it.
2.4.6.pass: Descriptive headings and labels let the user understand and navigate the structure.

2.4.7.fail: A keyboard user tabs across the page with no visible indicator and can't tell which control is focused.
2.4.7.pass: A clear focus ring shows exactly where the keyboard user is at all times.

2.4.8.fail: A user with a cognitive disability gets lost deep in the site with no breadcrumb or current-page marker.
2.4.8.pass: Breadcrumbs and a marked current page show the user where they are.

2.4.9.fail: A screen reader user reads a link out of context and still can't tell what "learn more" refers to.
2.4.9.pass: The link text alone identifies the destination — no context needed.

2.4.10.fail: A screen reader user faces a long wall of text with no headings to jump between.
2.4.10.pass: Section headings let the user skim and jump to the part they need.

2.4.11.fail: A keyboard user tabs to a field that scrolls behind a sticky header and disappears from view.
2.4.11.pass: The focused element stays at least partly visible, never fully hidden behind fixed content.

2.4.12.fail: Part of the focused control is clipped by a sticky bar, so the keyboard user can't see its full state.
2.4.12.pass: The entire focused element stays visible, with nothing covering it.

2.4.13.fail: A low-vision keyboard user can't perceive the thin, faint focus outline and loses their position.
2.4.13.pass: A thick, high-contrast focus indicator is easy to spot.

2.5.1.fail: A user with limited dexterity can only advance the gallery by swiping — a gesture they can't perform.
2.5.1.pass: Single-tap previous/next buttons offer a simple alternative to the swipe.

2.5.2.fail: A user with tremors accidentally presses Delete and it fires on touch-down with no chance to cancel.
2.5.2.pass: The action fires on release, so the user can slide off to abort a mistaken press.

2.5.3.fail: A speech-input user says "click Search" but the button's hidden name is "Submit", so nothing happens.
2.5.3.pass: The button's accessible name contains its visible label, so the voice command works.

2.5.4.fail: A user who can't shake their device — or shakes it involuntarily — can't trigger, or keeps mis-triggering, the shake action.
2.5.4.pass: An on-screen button does the same thing, and motion triggering can be disabled.

2.5.5.fail: A user with a tremor repeatedly misses a tiny 24px icon button.
2.5.5.pass: A 44px target is comfortably large enough to hit reliably.

2.5.6.fail: A user who attaches a keyboard to their tablet finds it disabled because the app assumed touch-only.
2.5.6.pass: Touch, mouse, and keyboard all work interchangeably, even mid-task.

2.5.7.fail: A user who can't perform a sustained drag can't reorder the list or move a kanban card.
2.5.7.pass: Up and down buttons let the user reorder without dragging.

2.5.8.fail: A touchscreen user with motor difficulties keeps tapping the wrong one of several cramped 16px buttons.
2.5.8.pass: Targets are at least 24px or well-spaced, so taps land where intended.

3.1.1.fail: With no lang attribute, the screen reader reads English text using French pronunciation rules — it comes out as gibberish.
3.1.1.pass: lang="en" tells the screen reader to use the right voice and pronunciation.

3.1.2.fail: A French phrase on an English page is read with English pronunciation and comes out garbled.
3.1.2.pass: The phrase is marked lang="fr", so the screen reader switches pronunciation just for it.

3.1.3.fail: A user unfamiliar with the jargon hits an undefined term and can't follow the rest.
3.1.3.pass: A definition or glossary link lets the user look up the unusual term.

3.1.4.fail: A user encounters "ARIA" with no expansion and doesn't know what it stands for.
3.1.4.pass: The expansion is available (e.g. via <abbr>), so the user can decode it.

3.1.5.fail: A user with limited literacy bounces off dense, complex legal prose.
3.1.5.pass: A plain-language summary makes the content understandable.

3.1.6.fail: A screen reader pronounces an ambiguous heteronym the wrong way, changing the meaning for the listener.
3.1.6.pass: A pronunciation cue (ruby or phonetics) disambiguates the word.

3.2.1.fail: A screen reader user tabs onto a dropdown and the page unexpectedly navigates away, disorienting them.
3.2.1.pass: Focusing a control changes nothing; navigation waits for an explicit action.

3.2.2.fail: A user selects a dropdown option and the page submits before they're done, with no warning.
3.2.2.pass: Changing a value does nothing surprising; a Go button triggers the change.

3.2.3.fail: A screen reader user re-learns the menu on every page because its order keeps changing.
3.2.3.pass: Navigation appears in the same order on every page, so it's predictable.

3.2.4.fail: The same action is called "Search" on one page and "Find" on another, so the user thinks they're different.
3.2.4.pass: The same function is labeled identically everywhere, so it's recognizable.

3.2.5.fail: The page auto-refreshes and yanks a screen reader user back to the top mid-read.
3.2.5.pass: Context changes only when the user asks, so nothing moves unexpectedly.

3.2.6.fail: A user in difficulty hunts for the contact link, which sits in a different place on each page.
3.2.6.pass: Help appears in the same place on every page, so it's easy to find.

3.3.1.fail: A colorblind user submits the form and only a red border marks the bad field — they can't tell what's wrong.
3.3.1.pass: A text message names the field and the problem, and it's announced to screen readers too.

3.3.2.fail: A screen reader user reaches a field whose only label was a placeholder that's now gone — they don't know what to enter.
3.3.2.pass: A persistent label and format hint tell the user exactly what's expected.

3.3.3.fail: The form says only "Invalid input", so the user keeps guessing and re-failing.
3.3.3.pass: The error suggests the fix — "enter the date as MM/DD/YYYY" — so the user can recover.

3.3.4.fail: A user with a cognitive disability submits a payment with a typo and there's no way to review or undo it.
3.3.4.pass: A review-and-confirm step lets the user catch the mistake before it's final.

3.3.5.fail: A user stuck on a complex field finds no hint or help anywhere nearby.
3.3.5.pass: Context-sensitive help sits right beside the field that needs it.

3.3.6.fail: Any form submits instantly with no checks, so a slip can't be caught.
3.3.6.pass: Validation and a confirm step give every form a safety net.

3.3.7.fail: A user with motor difficulty must re-type the same address a second time in the same checkout.
3.3.7.pass: A "same as billing" option reuses the earlier entry — no re-typing.

3.3.8.fail: A user with memory difficulties is blocked because login forbids paste and demands a solved puzzle.
3.3.8.pass: Password managers and paste work, so no memory test is required to log in.

3.3.9.fail: Login requires solving an object-recognition puzzle a user with a cognitive disability can't reliably pass.
3.3.9.pass: A passkey or magic link signs the user in with no cognitive test at all.

4.1.1.fail: Duplicate IDs make aria-labelledby point at the wrong element, so the screen reader announces a mismatched name.
4.1.1.pass: Unique IDs keep ARIA references intact, so names are announced correctly.

4.1.2.fail: A screen reader hits a div-based toggle and announces nothing — no name, no role, no on/off state.
4.1.2.pass: The control exposes its name, role, and state, so the screen reader says "Mute, toggle button, pressed".

4.1.3.fail: A blind user adds an item to the cart and hears nothing — the "Added" confirmation appears only on screen.
4.1.3.pass: A live region announces "Added to cart" without moving focus, so the user gets the feedback.
